summary.detailed_summary: PedSleepMAE proposes a transformer-based masked autoencoder trained on multimodal pediatric polysomnography (PSG) data, including EEG, EOG, EMG, and respiratory signals. Unlike previous supervised models, it uses self-supervised masked reconstruction to learn general-purpose embeddings that are flexible to missing channels. The model's utility is demonstrated through linear probing for sleep scoring and apnea detection, as well as its ability to impute missing channels and identify biomarkers for Prader-Willi Syndrome.

headline_results: Statistically significant separation of PWS patients in embedding space
Lower MSE/DTW for channel imputation compared to mean imputation baseline
tasks: Binary detection of apnea, hypopnea, EEG arousal, and oxygen desaturation

key_points: New EEG foundation model: A multimodal masked autoencoder (MAE) trained on pediatric PSG data to provide a general-purpose representation for sleep health.
Demonstrates versatility across multiple tasks, including 5-stage sleep scoring (71.3% F1) and the detection of apnea and oxygen desaturation via linear probing.
Capable of generative tasks such as missing channel imputation and the retrieval of representative sleep segments using embedding space arithmetic.
limitations: High inter-patient variability hinders the formation of distinct clusters in 2D UMAP space across different subjects.
Generated synthetic signals are described as noisy.
The effect size for Prader-Willi Syndrome biomarker detection is small, despite being statistically significant.
Evaluation of downstream tasks primarily relied on linear probing rather than full fine-tuning.

method.architecture: Transformer-based Masked Autoencoder (MAE) using Vision Transformer (ViT) blocks (3 layers, 4 heads) for both encoder and decoder.
method.finetuning: Downstream tasks are evaluated using linear probing (frozen encoder) or the decoder for signal generation and imputation.
method.objective: Self-supervised masked reconstruction (50% masking ratio, patch size 8) of multimodal PSG signals.
method.pretraining: Pre-trained on 2,379 pediatric PSG records using a masked reconstruction objective.

summary.one_liner: A multimodal masked autoencoder for pediatric sleep signals capable of sleep scoring, event detection, and signal imputation.
summary.title: PedSleepMAE: Generative Model for Multimodal Pediatric Sleep Signals
summary.unique_contribution: The first general-purpose generative model specifically designed for multimodal pediatric sleep signals, leveraging a masked autoencoder to learn representations for diverse clinical tasks.

papers.title: PedSleepMAE: Generative Model for Multimodal Pediatric Sleep Signals

reasons: EEG is a central modality within the multimodal pediatric sleep signals used.
Uses a masked autoencoder (MAE) to create a 'general-purpose generative model'.
Produces embeddings used for multiple diverse downstream tasks, including sleep scoring and genetic disorder analysis.
Explicitly claims to be the first general-purpose model for these signals, aligning with foundation model goals.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a neural foundation model for vision that leverages contrastive learning to align representations from EEG, MEG, and fMRI into a shared space anchored by a frozen CLIP image encoder. Unlike prior work focused on single modalities or tasks, this framework enables bidirectional mapping between neural activity and visual stimuli, as well as translation between different neural modalities. The approach is validated through image retrieval tasks (decoding), neural pattern prediction (encoding), and cross-modal translation, achieving high CLIP 2-way accuracy across all three modalities.

headline_results: fMRI to MEG conversion: 95.4% normalized accuracy

key_points: New new_model: Aligns EEG, MEG, and fMRI data into a shared representation space using a frozen CLIP image encoder.
Implements a bidirectional contrastive learning objective to support visual decoding, neural encoding, and translation between different brain imaging modalities.
Demonstrates strong performance in fMRI decoding (93.8% CLIP 2-way accuracy) and high fidelity in modality conversion (e.g., 95.4% normalized accuracy for fMRI to MEG).
limitations: The model is currently non-generative and relies on retrieval.
Performance is dependent on the availability of diverse, pre-existing datasets.
The framework is presented as a proof-of-concept rather than a fully scaled foundation model.

method.architecture: CNNs for EEG/MEG and MLPs for fMRI, consisting of a subject-specific alignment layer and a shared refinement network.
method.objective: Contrastive CLIP loss (maximizing cosine similarity for positive pairs).
method.pretraining: Aligning neural representations to a frozen CLIP Image encoder.

summary.one_liner: A multimodal framework that aligns EEG, MEG, and fMRI representations with CLIP image embeddings for visual decoding, encoding, and cross-modality conversion.
summary.title: Towards Neural Foundation Models for Vision: Aligning EEG, MEG, and fMRI Representations for Decoding, Encoding, and Modality Conversion
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a unified contrastive learning framework capable of simultaneous neural decoding, encoding, and cross-modality conversion across EEG, MEG, and fMRI.

papers.title: Towards Neural Foundation Models for Vision: Aligning EEG, MEG, and fMRI Representations for Decoding, Encoding, and Modality Conversion

reasons: Explicitly aims to create a 'foundational model' for aligning neural data
EEG is a core target modality alongside MEG and fMRI
Focuses on creating aligned representations for broad tasks including decoding, encoding, and modality conversion

summary.detailed_summary: This paper proposes EEG-GraphAdapter (EGA), a parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) approach designed to address the computational cost and data scarcity challenges in fine-tuning large-scale EEG foundation models for both temporal and spatial features. EGA is integrated into a pre-trained temporal backbone model (BENDR) as a GNN-based module, freezing the backbone and allowing only the adapter to be fine-tuned. This enables the effective acquisition of EEG spatial representations, significantly reducing computational overhead and data requirements. Experimental evaluations on two healthcare-related downstream tasks—Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) and Abnormality Detection (TUAB)—show that EGA improves performance by up to 16.1% in F1-score compared with the backbone BENDR model, highlighting its potential for scalable and accurate EEG-based predictions.

headline_results: Up to 16.1% F1-score improvement on TUAB

key_points: New EEG foundation model adapter: EEG-GraphAdapter (EGA) integrates GNN-based modules with pre-trained temporal backbone models to capture spatial relationships between EEG sensors.
Method novelty: EGA freezes the pre-trained backbone model and fine-tunes only the GNN adapter, achieving up to 16.1% improvement in F1-score on healthcare tasks while reducing computational cost.

Strong evidence: Experiments on MDD and TUAB datasets demonstrate EGA's effectiveness, with GAT-based EGA achieving 12.8% higher F1-score on MDD and GraphSAGE-based EGA achieving 16.1% improvement on TUAB compared to baseline BENDR.
limitations: Computational speedup was only 17% due to data loading overhead, not proportional to parameter reduction
Performance varies significantly depending on GNN architecture choice (GAT best for MDD, GraphSAGE best for TUAB)
Limited evaluation to only two healthcare tasks, broader applicability needs verification
Requires standard 10-20 electrode configuration preprocessing
Does not address potential catastrophic forgetting in backbone model

method.architecture: GNN-based adapter (GCN, GraphSAGE, or GAT) integrated with BENDR backbone
method.finetuning: Fine-tune only GNN adapter while freezing backbone
method.pretraining: BENDR pre-trained on large-scale EEG data

summary.one_liner: EEG-GraphAdapter (EGA) is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach that integrates a GNN-based module with pre-trained EEG foundation models to capture spatial relationships between EEG sensors.
summary.title: Graph Adapter of EEG Foundation Models for Parameter Efficient Fine Tuning
summary.unique_contribution: We propose EEG-GraphAdapter (EGA), a parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach that integrates a GNN-based module with pre-trained EEG foundation models to capture spatial relationships between EEG sensors while significantly reducing computational overhead and data requirements.

papers.title: Graph Adapter of EEG Foundation Models for Parameter Efficient Fine Tuning

reasons: Explicitly targets 'EEG Foundation Models' in the title.
Proposes a parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) approach specifically for pre-trained EEG backbone models.
Focuses on the EEG-FM ecosystem by improving the adaptation of the BENDR foundation model to downstream tasks.

summary.detailed_summary: This paper addresses the challenge of leveraging extensive unlabeled high-density EEG data to improve performance in scenarios with limited labeled low-density EEG data. The authors formulate this as a graph transfer learning and knowledge distillation problem, proposing EEG-DisGCMAE - a unified pre-trained graph contrastive masked autoencoder distiller. The approach introduces a novel unified graph self-supervised pre-training paradigm that seamlessly integrates graph contrastive pre-training with graph masked autoencoder pre-training. Additionally, they propose a graph topology distillation loss function that allows a lightweight student model trained on low-density data to learn from a teacher model trained on high-density data during both pre-training and fine-tuning. This method effectively handles missing electrodes through contrastive distillation and is validated across four classification tasks using two clinical EEG datasets.
key_points: New EEG foundation model: EEG-DisGCMAE that unifies graph contrastive and masked autoencoder pre-training for EEG analysis
Novel graph topology distillation loss enables effective knowledge transfer from high-density to low-density EEG data
Validated across four clinical classification tasks with significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods
limitations: Requires high-density EEG data for teacher model training
Performance depends on quality of graph construction from EEG signals
Limited to specific electrode configurations (10-20 system)
Computational complexity of graph pre-training
May not generalize well to EEG systems with significantly different electrode layouts

method.architecture: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) - both Graph Transformer and vanilla GCNs
method.finetuning: Downstream classification with Cross-Entropy loss and graph topology distillation
method.objective: Unified graph self-supervised pre-training combining contrastive and generative objectives

summary.one_liner: Unified graph pre-training framework combining contrastive and masked autoencoder methods for EEG distillation.
summary.title: Pre-Training Graph Contrastive Masked Autoencoders are Strong Distillers for EEG
summary.unique_contribution: The paper introduces the first unified graph pre-training framework that combines contrastive and masked autoencoder methods for EEG analysis, along with a novel graph topology distillation loss for transferring knowledge from high-density to low-density EEG data.

papers.title: Pre-Training Graph Contrastive Masked Autoencoders are Strong Distillers for EEG

reasons: Proposes a unified self-supervised pre-training paradigm combining graph contrastive learning and masked autoencoders for EEG.
Focuses on creating reusable representations to bridge the gap between high- and low-density EEG data.
Validates the pre-trained model across multiple classification tasks, demonstrating transferability.

summary.detailed_summary: GEFM addresses a key limitation in existing EEG foundation models by incorporating inter-channel relationships alongside temporal dynamics. The model combines Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with a masked autoencoder architecture, treating EEG channels as graph nodes with edge weights based on geodesic distances. Through extensive experiments across three downstream tasks using three GNN architectures (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) and two sequence length adjustment strategies, GEFM demonstrated consistent improvements over baseline methods, particularly when using GCN with edge weights and a linear layer for sequence adjustment.

headline_results: Consistent improvements across all three downstream tasks

key_points: New EEG foundation model: GEFM integrates Graph Neural Networks with masked autoencoder to capture both temporal dynamics and inter-channel relationships in EEG signals.
Method novelty: Uses geodesic-distance-based edge weights between EEG channels and employs GCN architecture with linear layer sequence adjustment for optimal performance.
Strong empirical evidence: Outperformed baseline BENDR model across all three downstream tasks (MMI, P300, ERN) with up to 31.4% improvement on MMI task.
limitations: Limited to 19 EEG channels from 10/20 system
Only tested on three specific downstream tasks
Sequence length adjustment may introduce information loss
GNN architectures without edge weight support showed poor performance
Limited comparison with other recent EEG foundation models like MAEEG and Neuro-GPT

method.architecture: Graph Neural Networks with masked autoencoder
method.pretraining: masked autoencoder pre-training with geodesic-distance-based edge weights

summary.one_liner: Graph-Enhanced EEG Foundation Model (GEFM) integrates Graph Neural Networks with a masked autoencoder to capture both temporal dynamics and inter-channel relationships in EEG signals.
summary.title: GEFM: Graph-Enhanced EEG Foundation Model
summary.unique_contribution: First foundation model for EEG that integrates Graph Neural Networks to capture inter-channel relationships alongside temporal dynamics, demonstrating superior performance across multiple downstream tasks.

reasons: Title explicitly identifies the work as an 'EEG Foundation Model'.
Abstract describes a pre-training approach using masked autoencoders on unlabeled data for broad transfer.
Model is evaluated across multiple downstream tasks to demonstrate its utility as a foundation model.
